title: Free Waymo rides in California? You can thank a regulatory quirk.
gist: A delayed California CPUC decision is blocking Waymo from expanding its service area and charging for rides in its new Chinese-made Zeekr Ojai vehicle, amid controversy over emergency response and unaccompanied-minor rides.

themes.theme: Regulatory friction as a growth constraint

insights.claim: A delayed California CPUC decision (extended to September 25) is blocking Waymo from expanding into new Northern and Southern California areas and from charging for rides in its new Ojai vehicle.
insights.speaker: WIRED
insights.so_what: California's dual DMV+CPUC permitting is a material gating factor on robotaxi expansion pace, a regulatory-risk input for AV timelines.
insights.locator: But a delayed decision from a state regulatory agency

insights.claim: Waymo's proposed expansion would span from Sea Ranch and Sacramento through Oakland to San Jose in the north, and past Los Angeles to the San Diego/Tijuana border in the south.
insights.speaker: WIRED
insights.so_what: The scale of the pending footprint shows Waymo's near-term California TAM once approvals clear.
insights.locator: Waymo applied to the CPUC in January

insights.claim: The review intensified after a labor union representing ride-hail drivers filed a complaint about Waymo transporting unaccompanied minors, and regulators sought details on emergency response after a December San Francisco power outage stranded more than 60 Waymos in traffic.
insights.speaker: WIRED
insights.so_what: Organized labor is using the regulatory process to slow AV deployment, a recurring headwind that raises compliance and PR costs.
insights.locator: But the process has been caught up

insights.claim: Waymo's Ojai, its first purpose-built driverless taxi, is manufactured by China's Zeekr and carries 13 cameras, six radar systems, and four lidar sensors.
insights.speaker: WIRED
insights.so_what: Sensor count and Chinese sourcing quantify the hardware stack and its geopolitical exposure for a US AV leader.
insights.locator: Waymo's Ojai is its first vehicle purpose-built

insights.claim: Waymo says the Zeekr-built Ojai avoids the upcoming ban on Chinese-connected automotive tech because Zeekr only makes the vehicle shell while the connected systems are built and added in the US.
insights.speaker: WIRED
insights.so_what: The shell-versus-connected-systems distinction is the compliance workaround that will be tested by the incoming ban, a regulatory risk to Waymo's fleet plan.
insights.locator: The car is manufactured by the Chinese company Zeekr
